EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds behind yet another arctic front reached speeds from 30 to 45 mph with gusts as high as 55 to 65 mph across southwest Minnesota.  When periods of light snowfall occurred that would total between one and two inches, gusty winds produced considerable near-blizzard and blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds were sustained from 40 to 42 mph with gusts as high as 64 mph near Beaver Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A push of dangerously cold air spread across the Upper Midwest behind a series of strong arctic fronts from January 21 through January 23. Wind chills dropped into the -40s across much of the region, with AXN in Alexandria reporting the coldest wind chill of -50F during this stretch of extreme cold. The strongest winds did not line up with the coolest temperatures, limiting wind chills from getting any colder than -50F. ||The peak of the coldest conditions occurred from the evening on the 22nd into the morning of the 23rd. Very cold air temperatures around -10 to -20 below continued until the morning of the 26th, though winds were generally at or below 10 mph, limiting wind chills from dropping below -35F. ||The lowest wind chills were:|-50F at AXN in Alexandria, MN.|-49F at STC in St Cloud, MN.|-49F at OWA in Owatonna, MN.|-49F at GHW in Glenwood, MN.|-47F at MSP in the Twin Cities, MN.|-47F at BDH in Willmar, MN.|-47F at CFE in Buffalo, MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KANE reported wind gusts as high as 25 mph during the early evening as air temperatures dropped from -15F to -20F overnight. The lowest measured wind chill was -44F at 2AM on the 23rd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: KMSP reported wind gusts as high as 35 mph during the early evening as air temperatures dropped from -15F to -21F overnight. The lowest measured wind chill was -47F at 2AM on the 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front resulted in gusty winds and light snow from the morning of the 16th through the afternoon of the 17th. Since there was little to no snowpack on the ground, any reductions in visibility depended on how heavy the falling snow was and how strong winds were. Conditions were generally worst in open, rural areas where the snowfall was able to blow around easier.||Winds gusted over 40 mph in parts of western and southern Minnesota, and generally under an inch of snow fell across the region. Visibilities dropped to 1/4 mile in several locations, though it was often brief due to the showery nature of the snowfall. Two counties, Stevens and Watonwan, experienced blizzard conditions, with prolonged reductions in visibility at or below 1/4 mile and very strong winds.||The strongest measured wind gusts from January 16-17 were:|44 mph at KMOX in Morris, MN.|43 mph at KULM in New Ulm, MN|43 mph at KRWF in Redwood Falls, MN.|43 mph at KJYG in St James, MN.|41 mph at KBBB in Benson, MN.|39 mph at KBDH in Willmar, MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds gusting to 35 mph and scattered snow showers resulted in periodic reductions in visibility as low as a 1/4 mile at times, though generally around 1 to 2 miles. The worst conditions were during the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning on January 21st, a weak winter system brought light snow to portions of southeast Minnesota. While accumulations were generally on the lighter side of around 2 inches or less across the region, a few vehicle accidents occurred which resulted in 7 combined non-life threatening injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Accumulating snow during the morning hours on January 21st resulted in snow covered roadways across Fillmore County. During this period of accumulating snow, a two vehicle collision occurred on Highway 52 near Chatfield resulting in 4 individuals receiving non-life threatening injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved across the Upper Midwest the afternoon of the 4th into the early morning of the 5th. This brought a wintry mix to the Northland with snow, sleet, and freezing drizzle. An area of heavy banded snow along the lake and terrain enhancement set up along the North Shore around Grand Marais and areas further inland in northern Cook County. This led to snowfall amounts of 5 to 8 inches in this area with upwards of 9 inches reported near Grand Marais in about 6 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow was heavier than expected across portions of northern Cook County. Totals ended up in the 5 to 7 inch range with a high report of 7.5 inches 14 miles east-southeast of Sea Gull Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second wave of wintry precipitation occurred 24 hours after a light mix of snow and freezing rain fell the day prior. This round was heavier and temperatures were right around freezing, leading to a mixture of freezing rain and rain. For most locations, the main period of freezing occurred overnight from the 5th into the 6th as rainfall rates were lighter. Rates then increased during the morning of the 6th which made it difficult for ice accretion to continue as temperatures hovered right around the freezing point. By the morning commute, most areas saw more of a crunchy slush on the ground as the heavier rain started to melt the coating of ice that developed overnight. The heaviest impacted area was western Hennepin, Wright, Anoka, and Chisago counties where temperatures were slightly cooler and the rain rates were not as heavy.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS at KHCD reported several hours of freezing precipitation starting during the evening of January 5th and lasting into the early morning of the 6th. CoCoRaHS observers reported freezing rain and ice covered sidewalks near Hutchinson and Glencoe.

Rates then increased during the morning of the 6th which made it difficult for ice accretion to continue as temperatures hovered right around the freezing point. By the morning commute, most areas saw more of a crunchy slush on the ground as the heavier rain started to melt the coating of ice that developed overnight. The heaviest impacted area was western Hennepin, Wright, Anoka, and Chisago counties where temperatures were slightly cooler and the rain rates were not as heavy.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observers reported a mix of light freezing rain and rain overnight that resulted in some icy spots where roads had not been treated near Zumbrota and Nerstrand.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds behind yet another arctic front reached speeds from 30 to 45 mph with gusts as high as 55 to 65 mph across southwest Minnesota.  When periods of light snowfall occurred that would total between one and two inches, gusty winds produced considerable near-blizzard and blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusted to 51 mph near Trosky and 44 mph at Pipestone. These winds combined with periods of light snowfall which totaled around 1 inch to produce visibility briefly as low as one-quarter to one-half mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper trough moved across the Northern Plains and into northwest Minnesota, bringing strong winds, light snow, and blizzard conditions. The initial area of light snow ended during the early morning of January 18 as a strong cold front moved into the region. Post frontal snow showers along with strong winds behind the front contributed to widespread whiteout conditions primarily across northeast North Dakota and the Red River Valley of northwest Minnesota. Strongest wind gusts were 50 to 57 mph. A no travel advised was issued by MNDOT in far northwest Minnesota due to whiteout conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibilities continuously below one quarter mile in falling snow and blowing snow, with winds frequently gusting 40 to 56 mph reported by mesonet site MN168 at Warren, MN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a period of strong winds and light snow just the day prior, many locations saw a second cold front move through the region, producing gusty winds and light snow from the morning of the 18th through the morning of the 19th. Since there was little to no snowpack on the ground, any reductions in visibility depended on how heavy the falling snow was and how strong winds were. Conditions were generally worst in open, rural areas where the snowfall was able to blow around easier.||Winds gusted up to 45 mph in parts of western and southern Minnesota, and generally under an inch of snow fell across the region. Visibilities dropped to 1/4 mile in several locations, though it was often brief due to the showery nature of the snowfall. The snowfall was heaviest before the strongest winds developed, limiting overall impacts from this event.||The strongest measured wind gusts from January 18-19 were:|46 mph at KULM in New Ulm, MN.|45 mph at KBDH in Willmar, MN.|44 mph at KAXN in Alexandria, MN.|44 mph at KDXX in Madison, MN.|44 mph at KJYG in St James, MN.|43 mph at KMOX in Morris, MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds gusting to 40 mph and scattered snow showers resulted in periodic reductions in visibility as low as a 1/2 mile at times, though generally remained around 1 to 2 miles. The worst conditions were during the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold high pressure settled in the Northern and Central Plains the morning of the 19th. With low pressure near the Straits of Mackinac, winds persisted through the night allowing wind chills to drop into the -40F to -45F range across portions of northeastern Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills as low as -44F were reported in the northern parts of Cook and Lake counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds behind yet another arctic front reached speeds from 30 to 45 mph with gusts as high as 55 to 65 mph across southwest Minnesota.  When periods of light snowfall occurred that would total between one and two inches, gusty winds produced considerable near-blizzard and blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusted to as high as 52 mph near Mountain Lake and 48 mph near Jeffers. These winds combined with periods of snow totaling 1 to 2 inches to produce periods with visibility of one-quarter mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration extreme cold every took place across the region starting the 22nd and lasting into the morning of the 24th. An area of very cold high pressure worked into the Plains on the 22nd and became established on the 23rd into the 24th. Air temperatures remain below zero during the event with light winds dropping wind chills into the 40s and 50s below zero. A few locations even dropped into the 60s below zero one morning with light winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills as low as -50F were reported in the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper-type system brought a period of strong winds and light to locally moderate snow accumulations that resulted in localized blizzard conditions within the Red River Valley of  northwest and west central Minnesota. Snow totals ranged from around inch to 3 inches. The highest wind gusts ranged from 45mph to 56 mph in northwest MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minnesota RWIS station at MN144, 1 mile north of Borup, reports blizzard conditions ongoing since 0510 CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air settled into the Northern Plains and Upper Midwest resulting in a prologued period of extreme cold and wind chills across northwest and west central Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KHCO AWOS at Hallock, MN frequently reported wind chills -40 or lower from the morning of January 22 through the early morning hours of January 26. There were a few shorter periods of improvements during the afternoon of January 24. The lowest wind chill value during this period at that site was -50.

Since there was little to no snowpack on the ground, any reductions in visibility depended on how heavy the falling snow was and how strong winds were. Conditions were generally worst in open, rural areas where the snowfall was able to blow around easier.||Winds gusted up to 45 mph in parts of western and southern Minnesota, and generally under an inch of snow fell across the region. Visibilities dropped to 1/4 mile in several locations, though it was often brief due to the showery nature of the snowfall. The snowfall was heaviest before the strongest winds developed, limiting overall impacts from this event.||The strongest measured wind gusts from January 18-19 were:|46 mph at KULM in New Ulm, MN.|45 mph at KBDH in Willmar, MN.|44 mph at KAXN in Alexandria, MN.|44 mph at KDXX in Madison, MN.|44 mph at KJYG in St James, MN.|43 mph at KMOX in Morris, MN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration extreme cold every took place across the region starting the 22nd and lasting into the morning of the 24th. An area of very cold high pressure worked into the Plains on the 22nd and became established on the 23rd into the 24th. Air temperatures remain below zero during the event with light winds dropping wind chills into the 40s and 50s below zero. A few locations even dropped into the 60s below zero one morning with light winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills as low as -46F were reported in the northern part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weather system resulted in a light wintry mix of precipitation during the early afternoon on January 4th. Surface temperatures were about 5 to 10 degrees below freezing, under a slightly warmer air mass just above the surface. This resulted in a few hours of light freezing rain or drizzle, and ultimately light ice accretion, creating slick surfaces for a number of counties in east-central Minnesota. Farther north, the airmass just above the surface was slightly cooler, resulting in more snow mixing in with any freezing precipitation. This limited impacts for areas north and west of St Cloud, Princeton, and Rush City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observers reported a glaze of ice near Monticello and some light sleet and freezing rain in Maple Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds surged behind a strong arctic cold front which moved across the region early on January 16. Winds persisted into the midday on January 17, with occasional gusts as high as 35 to 50 mph. Areas of light snowfall generally on the order of a dusting to an inch fell before and concurrent with the strong winds, and locations mainly near Highway 71 experienced considerable time with visibility near or below one quarter mile with travel not recommended in areas surrounding Windom.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 30 to 34 mph at times with a peak gust of 47 mph at a Minnesota Road Weather Information System site 2 miles north of Trosky and 46 mph at a RAWS site near Pipestone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong clipper system moved across northern Minnesota during the afternoon of the 15th. A few intense areas of snowfall developed ahead of a cold front and brought snow squalls that quickly reduced visibility and created quickly accumulating snow on roadways per area DOT webcams. This activity was mainly observed along the International Border area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds surged behind a strong arctic cold front which moved across the region early on January 16. Winds persisted into the midday on January 17, with occasional gusts as high as 35 to 50 mph. Areas of light snowfall generally on the order of a dusting to an inch fell before and concurrent with the strong winds, and locations mainly near Highway 71 experienced considerable time with visibility near or below one quarter mile with travel not recommended in areas surrounding Windom.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Occasional light snowfall totaled up to around one inch during a period of enhanced northwest wind gusts as high as 40 to 50 mph and led to a brief degradation in visibility to around one-quarter mile. With little or no pre-existing snow cover, impacts due to the lowest visibility periods were quite spotty and very brief.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second wave of wintry precipitation occurred 24 hours after a light mix of snow and freezing rain fell the day prior. This round was heavier and temperatures were right around freezing, leading to a mixture of freezing rain and rain. For most locations, the main period of freezing occurred overnight from the 5th into the 6th as rainfall rates were lighter. Rates then increased during the morning of the 6th which made it difficult for ice accretion to continue as temperatures hovered right around the freezing point. By the morning commute, most areas saw more of a crunchy slush on the ground as the heavier rain started to melt the coating of ice that developed overnight. The heaviest impacted area was western Hennepin, Wright, Anoka, and Chisago counties where temperatures were slightly cooler and the rain rates were not as heavy.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS at KMKT reported several hours of freezing precipitation during the evening of January 5th. Several CoCoRaHS observers reported freezing rain and ice covered sidewalks and driveways near Mankato, Lake Crystal, and Minnesota Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system brought light snowfall to the region the 15th into the 16th with some light snow showers lingering into the 17th. Northeasterly winds led to lake enhancement near Duluth along with some pockets of heavier snow observed across portions of St. Louis County. Accumulations of 5-7 inches were reported in these areas with a high report of 7.1 inches near Duluth.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals across much of the area were under 6 inches, but there were a few pockets of heavier snow. One of those pockets resulted in 6.6 inches of accumulation near Sea Gull Lake.

The strongest wind gusts and greatest snow amounts were measured across southwest and southern MN during the afternoon and evening, resulting in occasional periods of near-blizzard conditions with visibilities as low as 1/2 to 1/4 mile.||The strongest measured wind gusts on January 21 were:|42 mph at KMOX in Morris, MN.|41 mph at KCNB in Canby, MN.|40 mph at KSTP in St Paul, MN.|39 mph at KMIC in Crystal, MN.|39 mph at KRWF in Redwood Falls, MN.|38 mph at KFRM in Fairmont, MN.|38 mph at KULM in New Ulm, MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of very strong winds and light snow showers resulted in reduced visibility from blowing snow. The AWOS at KGBD noted visibility as low as 3/4 mile and wind gusts between 30 to 35 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system brought light snowfall to the region the 15th into the 16th with some light snow showers lingering into the 17th. Northeasterly winds led to lake enhancement near Duluth along with some pockets of heavier snow observed across portions of St. Louis County. Accumulations of 5-7 inches were reported in these areas with a high report of 7.1 inches near Duluth.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A long duration snowfall led to higher than expected snowfall accumulations in the Duluth area with this system. A few areas received upward of 7 inches of accumulation with a high report of 7.1 inches 12 miles north-northwest of Duluth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a period of strong winds and light snow just the day prior, many locations saw a second cold front move through the region, producing gusty winds and light snow from the morning of the 18th through the morning of the 19th. Since there was little to no snowpack on the ground, any reductions in visibility depended on how heavy the falling snow was and how strong winds were. Conditions were generally worst in open, rural areas where the snowfall was able to blow around easier.||Winds gusted up to 45 mph in parts of western and southern Minnesota, and generally under an inch of snow fell across the region. Visibilities dropped to 1/4 mile in several locations, though it was often brief due to the showery nature of the snowfall. The snowfall was heaviest before the strongest winds developed, limiting overall impacts from this event.||The strongest measured wind gusts from January 18-19 were:|46 mph at KULM in New Ulm, MN.|45 mph at KBDH in Willmar, MN.|44 mph at KAXN in Alexandria, MN.|44 mph at KDXX in Madison, MN.|44 mph at KJYG in St James, MN.|43 mph at KMOX in Morris, MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds gusting to 45 mph and scattered snow showers resulted in periodic reductions in visibility as low as a 1/4 mile at times, though generally remained around 1 to 2 miles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As Arctic high pressure moved into the area, a stiff northwest breeze resulted in some dangerous cold and wind chills for west-central Minnesota. The Wheaton ASOS and Minnesota Department of Transportation mesonet stations 3 miles west southwest of Graceville and 3 miles west of Odessa would all record wind chills as low as 45 degrees below zero.

As Arctic high pressure moved into the area, a stiff northwest breeze resulted in some dangerous cold and wind chills for west-central Minnesota. The Wheaton ASOS and Minnesota Department of Transportation mesonet stations 3 miles west southwest of Graceville and 3 miles west of Odessa would all record wind chills as low as 45 degrees below zero.

A strong upper trough moved across the Northern Plains and into northwest Minnesota, bringing strong winds, light snow, and blizzard conditions. The initial area of light snow ended during the early morning of January 18 as a strong cold front moved into the region. Post frontal snow showers along with strong winds behind the front contributed to widespread whiteout conditions primarily across northeast North Dakota and the Red River Valley of northwest Minnesota. Strongest wind gusts were 50 to 57 mph. A no travel advised was issued by MNDOT in far northwest Minnesota due to whiteout conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibilities continuously below one quarter mile in falling snow and blowing snow, with winds frequently gusting 40 to 53 mph reported by mesonet site MN057 at Saint Vincent, MN. Whiteout conditions and frequent gusts over 35 mph were also reported at MN024 at Donaldson, MN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second wave of wintry precipitation occurred 24 hours after a light mix of snow and freezing rain fell the day prior. This round was heavier and temperatures were right around freezing, leading to a mixture of freezing rain and rain. For most locations, the main period of freezing occurred overnight from the 5th into the 6th as rainfall rates were lighter. Rates then increased during the morning of the 6th which made it difficult for ice accretion to continue as temperatures hovered right around the freezing point. By the morning commute, most areas saw more of a crunchy slush on the ground as the heavier rain started to melt the coating of ice that developed overnight. The heaviest impacted area was western Hennepin, Wright, Anoka, and Chisago counties where temperatures were slightly cooler and the rain rates were not as heavy.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at KSTP in St Paul reported several hours of freezing rain and rain starting late on January 5th and lasting into the early morning of the 6th. CoCoRaHS observers reported a mix of freezing rain and drizzle leading to icy sidewalks and driveways. Warming temperatures and heavier rainfall rates later in the morning allowed the ice to partially melt by the morning commute.
